summary: King County Executive Dow Constantine played a significant role during the pandemic, especially regarding budget decisions. In 2020, he was hesitant to approve the contract for frontline workers like us because of budgetary issues. Despite union efforts and support from the Seattle City Council, he pushed for cuts, which led to layoffs of part-time workers in August. It felt unfair that frontline workers were being targeted for reductions during such a critical time.

The Seattle City Council was an important ally for us during the pandemic, especially in contract negotiations. When the King County Executive tried to back out of agreed terms due to budget problems, the Council mostly supported keeping the contract intact. I remember there was a strong letter-writing campaign from union members to convince them. Nearly everyone on the Council voted to maintain our cost of living raises and contract terms, which was a big win for us.

King County is where I work as a transit operator, and it was deeply affected by the pandemic. The county faced significant budget challenges, which led to service cuts and layoffs, including 2,200 part-time workers in August 2020. These were unprecedented layoffs for Metro, which had never done that before. The county's decisions directly impacted our work conditions and job security during this difficult time.

entity: American Federation of State, County and Municipal Employees
summary: I am a member of the American Federation of State, County and Municipal Employees (AFSCME), which has been crucial in advocating for our safety and rights during the pandemic. The union fought hard to ensure we had protective measures like mask mandates and shields, although some of these took longer than they should to be implemented. They also pushed for hazard pay and contract protections, even when the county tried to backtrack. Overall, AFSCME has been our voice in these challenging times.

Seattle is where I live and work, and it felt like the epicenter of the COVID-19 pandemic early on. Starting around March 2020, transit services were cut significantly, and safety protocols like mask-wearing and rear boarding were quickly put in place. The city’s public officials, including the Seattle City Council, were involved in decisions affecting us frontline workers. Despite the challenges, Seattle’s diverse community of transit workers showed resilience and gradually adapted to the new safety norms.

I visited Spokane, Washington, in August 2020 and noticed they had implemented safety shields on buses much earlier than we had in Seattle. They were boarding passengers at the front again, which was different from our rear boarding policy. It was surprising because Seattle was considered a hotbed of the pandemic, yet Spokane seemed to have better protective equipment in place. I’m not sure if it’s because of differences in union representation or local policies, but it stood out to me.

The COVID-19 pandemic changed my work drastically starting in March 2020. Routes were cut, mask-wearing became mandatory, and we switched to rear boarding to reduce exposure. I felt moderately at risk because I’m in close quarters with the public, but I took extra precautions like wiping down my work area. The union fought hard for our safety, though some protective measures like shields took months to be tested and approved. Overall, the pandemic highlighted both the challenges and the importance of frontline transit workers.
